• American Mechanical Services (HVAC contractor) operates in CA, CO, TX, IN, and DC with 500+ employees; Patrick Collier is the Corporate Safety Director. • Primary need: improve safety education quality and ensure reliable, centralized training documentation for regulatory compliance across a dispersed workforce. • Trigger: past OSHA-related documentation issues in California and Maryland highlighted gaps in proof of training. • Vendor search: led by Patrick via MCAA peer recommendations and supplemental research; evaluated multiple platforms with hands-on trials and pricing/terms reviews. • Key criteria: mobile-friendly, easy-to-use interface; later involved ops managers and department heads for consensus testing. • Shortlist: KPA (selected), Tyfoom, and Safety Sites; Safety Sites deemed average and pricier than KPA. • Decision trade-off: Tyfoom’s content considered superior (short, human-led videos and quick tests) but was 2–3x the price; had pricing been closer, Patrick would likely have chosen Tyfoom. • Implementation: initial onboarding challenges (device compatibility, user tech savviness), then stable usage; used for new-hire onboarding and 4–5 monthly trainings company-wide. • Outcomes and issues: improved documentation and access to certificates/training history; major concerns with KPA content

tech savviness), then stable usage; used for new-hire onboarding and 4–5 monthly trainings company-wide. • Outcomes and issues: improved documentation and access to certificates/training history; major concerns with KPA content being stale, slideshow-style, glitchy, limited library/variety, long modules, non-instructive quiz retakes; platform has overly complex admin settings that can cause unintended changes. • Support and forward look: monthly cadence with a capable tech support rep; content feedback hasn’t translated to changes; if content quality/variety and updates don’t improve, AMS may switch vendors despite otherwise acceptable service.

(400 employees) is the second-largest U.S. porcelain tile producer; Julie is the EHS Project Manager seeking an ISO-aligned EHS system to replace paper-based training and documentation. • Goals: maintain ISO certifications, centralize training records, enable job hazard assessments and document control, and improve safety culture using leading/lagging indicators (injuries, near-misses, workers’ comp). • Vendor search included three options: EHS Insight (~$70k/yr, dismissed), Intelex (~$15k/yr for a smaller package), and KPA/Novara ($4,400/yr + one-time $15k implementation). • Key selection drivers: lowest total cost, ability to operate without individual logins for all 400 employees (admin-managed entries for group trainings), and strong ISO framework alignment. • Evaluation process: multiple demos and hands-on sessions; sales rep Cliff involved early; implementation team (Haley Ann and Jennifer) engaged pre-sale, which built trust. • Post-sale issue: training module—identified as a top requirement—was missing despite prior assurances; KPA initially quoted an extra $3,600/yr, causing a stalemate and ~3-week delay. • Escalation/resolution: Manager Scott acknowledged a “missed line item” and added the training module at $0 now and in future years; however, access was still not

stalemate and ~3-week delay. • Escalation/resolution: Manager Scott acknowledged a “missed line item” and added the training module at $0 now and in future years; however, access was still not enabled as of the latest check. • Impact: onboarding momentum stalled; missed weeks of training with the implementation team, internal frustration due to slow, unclear sales-side communications. • Current state: weekly Wednesday build sessions continue; relationship with the implementation team is strong; overall sentiment is cautiously positive but marked by early “red flag” concerns. • Counterfactuals: Without the no-cost training resolution, they would have walked and likely revisited Intelex; relationship quality and access to technical builders were influential beyond price.

• Hazen Research is a single-site, 140-employee metallurgical R&D lab with 15 buildings, serving global mining/minerals clients; they run onsite labs, perform mineralogy/thermal extraction work, and design/build custom processing equipment. • Brian McGuire, Environmental Health and Safety Manager (hired ~1.5 years ago), drove the push to adopt an EHS platform after finding manual, inconsistent Excel/Word tracking and underreported incidents. • A cross-functional selection team included the Radiation Safety Officer, Environmental Manager, HR Manager, a Mineralogy director, and a lab IT specialist. • Formal RFP process: used Capterra to narrow 598 products to 36, then 17, then 5 finalists via criteria and “deal killers.” • Key requirements: incident and hazard reporting with action tracking; asset tracking; robust SDS management for thousands of chemicals; easy, largely out-of-the-box setup; strong mobile app; built-in training platform with content; affordability. • Finalists: KPA, Velocity EHS, HSI, Aqaline (dropped for being France-based), Contractor Foreman (dropped for contractor focus); also reconsidered Chemical Safety (excellent SDS but no training content, thus ruled out). • KPA won over Velocity mainly due to easier customization of forms (especially incident reports),

contractor focus); also reconsidered Chemical Safety (excellent SDS but no training content, thus ruled out). • KPA won over Velocity mainly due to easier customization of forms (especially incident reports), adequate asset module, better/more relatable training content (real people vs Velocity’s CGI/AI “stick-figure” style), strong mobile app and QR codes, and overall ease of use. • Price: KPA was slightly higher than Velocity, but leadership approved given value; ballpark expectation was ~$30k, especially reasonable compared to other recent systems (e.g., LIMS ~$100k/yr). • Implementation was rapid: purchased late Oct, live by Dec 1, broadly adopted by Jan 1; Brian’s prior KPA experience and the RSO’s ownership sped setup; preloaded 2025 data enabled quick analytics that impressed leadership. • Feedback: consider live app demos via phone mirroring during sales; improve in-platform charts/graphics (currently basic—users export to Excel); Novara spin-off was a surprise but has had no negative impact; local Colorado support was a plus.

• Hercules Industries is an employee-owned (ESOP) sheet metal/HVAC manufacturer-distributor with 32 locations and ~650–680 employees; Danny (25 years) leads safety, Kirk (18 years) supports operations/safety. • They sought an EHS platform to replace paper, spreadsheets, and an outdated OSHA 300/300A system, aiming to scale with growth toward ~1,000 employees and avoid violations proactively. • Selection was led by Danny alone, informed by conferences (e.g., National Safety Council) and their self-insured captive’s vendor partners; prior familiarity with vendors aided shortlisting. • Must-haves: OSHA recordkeeping (300/300A), incident documentation, and major time savings through digitized inspections (forklifts, branch walkthroughs) and automated workflows across many locations. • KPA/Novara stood out for cost flexibility, customization options, and a broad platform roadmap (e.g., driver files, digital reports), with potential to consolidate other tools (e.g., SDS currently with Velocity EHS). • Budget was capped at $15K/year; KPA/Novara met it via configurable program scope, while other vendors were less flexible (user/feature constraints). • Sales process was responsive and informative, with tailored demos branded for Hercules; suggestion: provide clearer, itemized pricing for add-ons (training, SDS, etc.) to aid

were less flexible (user/feature constraints). • Sales process was responsive and informative, with tailored demos branded for Hercules; suggestion: provide clearer, itemized pricing for add-ons (training, SDS, etc.) to aid planning. • Onboarding occurred during vendor transition (KPA to Novara), causing delayed kickoff; weekly sessions felt fast—request for more frequent early sessions and richer self-help/how-to video library. • Go-live: Feb 1; early benefits include streamlined data aggregation and reduced manual handling; still tuning notifications and ownership contacts post-transition (unclear Novara point person). • Product feedback/roadmap: need asset-level location filters, last-inspected flags to prevent duplicate daily inspections, and clearer notification targeting; overall sentiment is positive and optimistic once processes are fully dialed in.

• J-W Power is an oil and gas services company with ~600 employees across 35 field offices in 13 states; Travis is the Director of Safety. • In 2021, they sought to replace a 10-year-old, home-built Caspio-based EHS tool that lacked robustness and data depth. • Core use cases: incident reporting, safety observations (near-misses, BBS, hazard IDs), and tracking leading/lagging indicators like TRIR. • Evaluation process: Travis led an RFI-style, multi-vendor review (about six vendors) with IT, safety, and operations stakeholders; initial 1-hour demos, refined requirements, pricing, and deeper demos/sandboxes. • KPA (then iScout/Flex) beat Velocity EHS due to superior flexibility, ease of use, self-serve form/workflow creation, and oilfield domain fit; pricing was competitive but not a deciding factor. • Sales experience was strong—knowledgeable team (notably Elena) who translated problems into workable configurations without being pushy. • Onboarding and rollout were excellent: KPA built initial forms while enabling self-sufficiency; high field adoption via an easy mobile UI. • Post-implementation (years 2–3) support dipped amid KPA acquisition: account manager changes, slow/unclear escalation, occasional incorrect “can’t do” answers, and delayed SDS module

field adoption via an easy mobile UI. • Post-implementation (years 2–3) support dipped amid KPA acquisition: account manager changes, slow/unclear escalation, occasional incorrect “can’t do” answers, and delayed SDS module pricing engagement. • Current state is improved: faster responses, strong new account rep (Dale), and high internal self-sufficiency; USA Compression acquired J-W and uses Velocity, but is open to KPA—KPA’s Acknowledgments feature impressed them. • Product feedback: preserve hyperlinks in spreadsheet exports, enable dashboard drill-down to underlying records, and add a monthly/quarterly account touchpoint cadence; overall, Travis remains a strong advocate of KPA.

• Keywell Metals is a recycler of aerospace-grade metals (titanium, high-alloy steels like Inconel 718), processing skeletonized scrap into clean, furnace-ready material; ~120 employees across 3 sites (2 near Charlotte, NC; 1 in Falconer, NY). • Chris is the Director of HSE for all sites; he sought to modernize antiquated, paper-based processes (even payroll) and enable historical analysis without filing cabinets. • Core requirements: digital/mobile inspections and checklists; robust SDS/chemical management; e-learning/training; HR policy acknowledgments with signatures; and QMS support for ISO 9001/AS9100 traceability and nonconformance tracking. • He evaluated 23 EHS platforms via demos and materials, quickly disqualifying many that lacked needed safety, quality, or inspection capabilities; finalists were KPA (Novara) and VelocityEHS. • Both finalists were usable and feature-rich (templates, libraries aligned to OSHA 1910/1926), but KPA checked more of Keywell’s boxes overall. • Decision drivers: budget mattered (~60%), KPA’s tiered seat pricing fit a small org better than Velocity’s all-in pricing; chose KPA despite being more expensive due to stronger perceived service and support. • KPA’s sales experience stood out: proactive, non-pushy weekly follow-ups, rapid answers, and

than Velocity’s all-in pricing; chose KPA despite being more expensive due to stronger perceived service and support. • KPA’s sales experience stood out: proactive, non-pushy weekly follow-ups, rapid answers, and access to subject-matter experts; online reviews suggested fewer/less severe support issues vs. Velocity. • Implementation began Q4 2025; went live Jan 2; onboarding/support were timely and effective; internal team’s systems expertise enabled rapid buildout; phased rollout (leadership to managers) is driving transparency and accountability. • KPIs and benefits: training compliance; preventive maintenance scheduling via inspections (e.g., forklift hour-based service prompts); OSHA metrics (TRIR, DART, 300/300A); projected ROI from avoided equipment failures (e.g., prior $15k forklift hydraulic repair ≈ annual KPA cost). • Gaps/cons: no native incident investigation/root-cause module tied to OSHA records (workaround via custom forms and workers’ comp portal); system date-stamps on submission rather than occurrence/completion date, causing schedule drift and potential OSHA dating inaccuracies; overall satisfaction remains high; special praise for sales rep Ethan Shapiro.
